burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with father Pope of Yale and daughter Aurelia

narrative_blocks.header: Twin brother John died at nineteen, 1803
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1 September 1784 at Greenfield Hill CT, he was the twin of John Dwight who died at age 19 in 1803 — leaving him the only surviving male of that birth-day pair among Pope Dwight's children. The twin's death came when James was just nineteen and starting his hardware apprenticeship.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant from New Haven to Petersburg
narrative_blocks.body: He went into hardware business with his older brother Timothy Dwight Jr at New Haven, then carried the trade to Petersburg VA. He returned to New York and established the hardware firm James & George A. Dwight before moving south again later in life to Georgia.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his first wife at twenty-five, 1813
narrative_blocks.body: On 13 March 1811 at New Haven he married Aurelia Darling, daughter of Joseph and Amelia Mills Darling. She bore him one daughter Elizabeth Smith Dwight in 1812 and died in 1813 at age 25 — leaving him a widower with an infant. He remarried 8 August 1815 at Norwich CT to Susan Breed.

narrative_blocks.header: Retired to New Haven, 1854
narrative_blocks.body: After decades of hardware trade between Connecticut, Virginia, New York, and Georgia, he returned to New Haven in 1854 at age 70 and retired there. He died 24 March 1863 at 78 — outliving by 60 years his twin brother John who had died at 19. Father of Tim V the 12th Yale president by Susan Breed.

career.location: New Haven CT → Petersburg VA → New York → Georgia
career.notes: Hardware partnership with brother Timothy Dwight Jr starting at New Haven; carried trade to Petersburg VA; established James & George A. Dwight hardware firm in NYC; later returned south to Georgia. Retired to New Haven 1854.

children.n: Timothy Dwight (infant son of James III)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with father Pope of Yale and daughter Aurelia

narrative_blocks.header: Twin brother John died at nineteen, 1803
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1 September 1784 at Greenfield Hill CT, he was the twin of John Dwight who died at age 19 in 1803 — leaving him the only surviving male of that birth-day pair among Pope Dwight's children. The twin's death came when James was just nineteen and starting his hardware apprenticeship.

narrative_blocks.header: Hardware merchant from New Haven to Petersburg
narrative_blocks.body: He went into hardware business with his older brother Timothy Dwight Jr at New Haven, then carried the trade to Petersburg VA. He returned to New York and established the hardware firm James & George A. Dwight before moving south again later in life to Georgia.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost his first wife at twenty-five, 1813
narrative_blocks.body: On 13 March 1811 at New Haven he married Aurelia Darling, daughter of Joseph and Amelia Mills Darling. She bore him one daughter Elizabeth Smith Dwight in 1812 and died in 1813 at age 25 — leaving him a widower with an infant. He remarried 8 August 1815 at Norwich CT to Susan Breed.

narrative_blocks.header: Retired to New Haven, 1854
narrative_blocks.body: After decades of hardware trade between Connecticut, Virginia, New York, and Georgia, he returned to New Haven in 1854 at age 70 and retired there. He died 24 March 1863 at 78 — outliving by 60 years his twin brother John who had died at 19. Father of Tim V the 12th Yale president by Susan Breed.

career.location: New Haven CT → Petersburg VA → New York → Georgia
career.notes: Hardware partnership with brother Timothy Dwight Jr starting at New Haven; carried trade to Petersburg VA; established James & George A. Dwight hardware firm in NYC; later returned south to Georgia. Retired to New Haven 1854.

narrative_blocks.header: Married a New Haven hardware merchant at twenty-three
narrative_blocks.body: Daughter of Joseph Darling and Amelia Mills Darling of Connecticut, she married James Dwight III on 13 March 1811 at New Haven at age twenty-three. He was twenty-six, fresh from a Yale education and starting his hardware business with his older brother.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at twenty-five leaving infant Elizabeth
narrative_blocks.body: She bore James one daughter Elizabeth Smith Dwight in 1812 and died in 1813 at twenty-five. James remarried two years later to Susan Breed in 1815; the infant Elizabeth was raised by her stepmother and grew to marry into the Bradley family before her own early death at thirty-six in 1848.

narrative_blocks.header: Motherless at one, half-sister to four Dwights
narrative_blocks.body: Her mother Aurelia Darling died in 1813 when she was one year old. Her father James Dwight III remarried Susan Breed in 1815 and had five more children; Elizabeth was raised with the half-Dwight siblings until her own death at thirty-six in 1848.

narrative_blocks.header: Lost her mother at one, raised by stepmother
narrative_blocks.body: Born 1812 in New Haven CT to James Dwight III and his first wife Aurelia Darling, she lost her mother at age one when Aurelia died in 1813. Raised by her stepmother Susan Breed Dwight alongside her five half-siblings, she died at thirty-six in 1848 — half a generation before her father's 1863 death.

parents.father_name: Breed family of New Haven CT

parents.mother_name: Susan (Breed) Dwight of New Haven CT

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with parents and Pope of Yale grandfather

narrative_blocks.header: Pope of Yale was her grandfather
narrative_blocks.body: Her father James Dwight III was the third son of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV — the eighth President of Yale College, known to his contemporaries as 'Pope Dwight.' Through her great-grandmother Mary Edwards Dwight (daughter of theologian Jonathan Edwards) she also carried the Yale Edwards line back to Northampton.

narrative_blocks.header: Cousin marriage united two Hooker descents
narrative_blocks.body: Her 1846 marriage to Rev. Richard Hooker brought together two seventh- and eighth-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker through entirely different lines — Richard via direct paternal John Hooker descent, Aurelia via Mary Hooker's 1690s marriage to Rev. James Pierpont and the subsequent Edwards-Dwight succession.

narrative_blocks.header: Survived husband by seventeen years
narrative_blocks.body: Widowed at forty-one in December 1857 when Richard died at Middletown, she returned to her New Haven family home and lived another seventeen years. She died there in January 1874 at fifty-seven and was buried at Grove Street Cemetery near her Dwight grandparents and parents.

notable.notable_blurb: uncle to the medical-textbook author Dr. Worthington Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Springfield Cemetery, Hampden County MA — returned to family burial ground

education.notes: Yale 1827 — youngest of the four Hooker brothers to attend Yale (John 1810, George 1814, Worthington 1825, Richard 1827).

career.notes: Settled in Macon Georgia after Yale and ordination; ministered there for several decades. His son Thomas was born at Macon in 1849.

narrative_blocks.header: Headed south after Yale, settled in Macon
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1827 he was ordained Presbyterian minister and settled in Macon, Bibb County, Georgia — the southernmost outpost of any Hooker brother of his generation. His three older brothers stayed close to Connecticut and Massachusetts in medicine and law; Richard took the gospel south.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Pope of Yale's granddaughter at thirty-eight
narrative_blocks.body: On 15 July 1846 he married Aurelia Dwight at New Haven CT — daughter of James Dwight III and granddaughter of Rev. Timothy Dwight IV, the eighth president of Yale College. The marriage came eighteen years after his Yale graduation and uncle Worthington's later Yale professorship.

narrative_blocks.header: Their son carried two Hooker lines at once
narrative_blocks.body: Their only child Thomas was born at Macon GA in September 1849 — a dual-line Hooker descendant through both parents. From his father Thomas inherited the direct John Hooker descent; from his mother, descent via Mary Hooker's marriage to Rev. James Pierpont in 1690s New Haven, through Sarah Pierpont's marriage to theologian Jonathan Edwards, through Mary Edwards Dwight to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Died at forty-nine in Middletown, buried home
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Middletown, Connecticut on 19 December 1857 at age forty-nine, while north on a visit from Macon. His body was returned to Springfield Cemetery in Hampden County MA to lie with his Hooker parents and Dwight grandparents.

narrative_blocks.header: Posthumously, brother-in-law became Yale's twelfth president
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Aurelia's younger brother Timothy Dwight V was eighteen at the time of Richard's 1846 marriage to her and twenty-nine when Richard died in 1857. Twenty-nine years after Richard's death, Timothy became the twelfth President of Yale in 1886 — the Hooker-Dwight family's second Yale presidency in three generations after Pope Dwight 1795-1817.

cross_connections.display_label: his father-in-law, the famed Springfield publisher

bio.notes: Named "Thomas" — honoring the founding ancestor Thomas Hooker (H00001)

narrative_blocks.header: Carried Thomas Hooker's blood through both parents
narrative_blocks.body: Born in Macon Georgia in September 1849 to Rev. Richard Hooker and Aurelia Dwight, he was the third documented child of a Hooker-Hooker cousin marriage in the tree — and the first whose dual descent propagated biologically. Through his father he descended directly from Thomas Hooker via the John Hooker line of Northampton; through his mother via Mary Hooker Pierpont's 1690s marriage and the Edwards-Dwight succession to Pope of Yale.

narrative_blocks.header: Macon-born, married a Springfield Republican daughter
narrative_blocks.body: He was born at Macon Georgia in September 1849, the only child of a Presbyterian minister and a Dwight bride. In 1874 he married Sarah Augusta 'Sally' Bowles of Springfield Massachusetts — daughter of Samuel Bowles III, the editor of the Springfield Republican newspaper, then one of America's most influential dailies.

narrative_blocks.header: Both sons graduated Yale, younger took Harvard Law
narrative_blocks.body: His daughter Aurelia died at twenty-four in 1899; his elder son Richard graduated Yale in 1899 the same year of her death; his younger son Thomas Jr graduated Yale in 1903 and went on to Harvard Law in 1906. He himself attended Yale's Sheffield Scientific School for two years without completing the degree.

narrative_blocks.header: Twenty-five years in New Haven banking and trusts
narrative_blocks.body: Returning to New Haven in 1877 after five years of study and travel abroad, he entered business and financial life. He became vice president of the New Haven Trust Company in 1895, its president from 1902 to 1909, and at the merger that created Union & New Haven Trust Company he stepped to vice president there — while simultaneously rising at the First National Bank to vice president 1902, president 1909.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with mother Aurelia, wife Sally, and Pope Dwight forebears

education.notes: Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven for early education — same school where his Dwight great-great-grandfather Pope Dwight had taught after his 1769 Yale graduation.
Yale BA 1869 per Carrington 1918 biography.
Yale MA 1872 — three years after BA.
Five years 1872-1877 of study and travel abroad and in this country; returned to New Haven 1877 to enter business and financial career.

career.notes: Vice president of New Haven Trust Company 1895-1902.
Joined First National Bank 1895.
President of New Haven Trust Company 1902-1909, until its merger into Union & New Haven Trust Company.
VP First National Bank 1902-1909.
President of First National Bank from 1909 — held position at time of Carrington 1918 biography.
VP of Union & New Haven Trust Company from 1909 (after merger of NH Trust Co).
Ten years on New Haven Board of Education, 1894-1904.

bio.display_name: Timothy Dwight (infant son of James III)

narrative_blocks.header: Timothy died young
narrative_blocks.body: Timothy died in 1822, aged about two. His parents gave the name to a brother born six years later.

death.cause: Died of wounds received while breaking up a student brawl as Yale tutor

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Yale 1840 — graduated at age 18-19, appointed tutor at Yale shortly after.

career.notes: Appointed Yale tutor; on his second day in role was wounded breaking up a student brawl caused by drunkenness.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor killed breaking up student brawl
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale 1840 at age eighteen and was appointed a tutor at the college. Two days into the role he was wounded while breaking up a student brawl caused by drunkenness, then died twenty days later on 20 October 1843 in New Haven at age twenty-one.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried his mother's Breed maiden name forward
narrative_blocks.body: He was the second son of James Dwight III and Susan Breed of Stonington CT, carrying her maiden name as his middle name. He died three years before his elder sister Aurelia married Rev. Richard Hooker in 1846.

death.cause: Complications from broken thigh sustained in dressing-room fall, 23 Jun 1897

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Yale 1846.
Taught a year or two at Brainard Academy in Haddam CT after Yale.
Yale Tutor 1849-1853.
Began theology study at Andover Seminary 1854.
Removed to Yale Divinity School 1856.
LL.B. Columbia 1861 under cousin Professor Theodore W. Dwight (founder of Columbia Law).

career.notes: Admitted to NY bar 1861.
Taught Municipal Law at Columbia 1863-1866 under cousin Prof. Theodore W. Dwight.

narrative_blocks.header: Yale tutor, Andover theology, Columbia Law
narrative_blocks.body: After Yale 1846 he tutored at Yale 1849-1853, studied theology at Andover Seminary then Yale Divinity, before taking his LL.B. at Columbia Law in 1861 under his cousin Professor Theodore W. Dwight — founder of Columbia Law School. He practiced law in New York City and assisted Theodore at Columbia teaching Municipal Law from 1863 to 1866.

narrative_blocks.header: Only child born and died same day, London
narrative_blocks.body: He married Cora Charlesina Tallmadge of New York in Philadelphia on 6 June 1866 at age forty. Their only child James McLaren Dwight was born — and died — on 4 May 1872 in London during the couple's extended European travels of 1869-1876.

narrative_blocks.header: Tripped in dressing room, died five days later
narrative_blocks.body: On the evening of 23 June 1897 he tripped and fell in his dressing room, breaking a thigh. The shock to his seventy-one-year-old system was such that he failed rapidly and died five days later on 1 July, just shy of his seventy-second birthday.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

parents.father_name: Maj. Charles B. (or Benjamin) Tallmadge, US Army

narrative_blocks.header: Outlived husband seventeen years after losing infant son
narrative_blocks.body: She married James McLaren Breed Dwight at Philadelphia on 6 June 1866 at age thirty-two. Their only child was born and died on 4 May 1872 in London during their European travels; she was widowed in 1897 and lived another seventeen years, dying at New Haven in 1914 at age eighty.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — body returned across Atlantic for burial in father's family plot

narrative_blocks.header: Born and died same day in London, 1872
narrative_blocks.body: He was born in London on 4 May 1872 during his parents' extended European travel and died the same day. His body was returned across the Atlantic to be buried at Grove Street Cemetery in his father's family plot in New Haven.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with father James Dwight III and grandfather Pope of Yale

parents.mother_research_notes: Susan Breed Dwight — daughter of John McLaren Breed and his second wife Rebecca (Walker) Breed. Her maternal grandfather Robert Walker was a judge of the Connecticut Superior Court. The Breed line brought additional CT judicial heritage into Tim V's ancestry beyond the Edwards-Dwight intellectual line on his father's side.

education.notes: Yale 1849 — entered at age 16, graduated top of class. Prizes in mathematics and Latin. Member of Linonian Society, Phi Beta Kappa, and Skull and Bones.
Clark Scholar 1849-1851, graduate work at Yale.
Studied at Yale Theological Department 1851-1853.
Studied at University of Bonn 1855-1856.
Studied at University of Berlin 1856-1858.

career.notes: Professor 1858-1886.
12th President 1886-1899. Yale became a university in name during his tenure (long having been one in fact).

career.organization: National Society Sons of the American Revolution
career.notes: Elected first chaplain at the organization's founding meeting at Fraunces Tavern, 19 April 1889.

institutions.blurb: Approached widow Maria Bissell Hotchkiss with idea to finance Yale prep school; original incorporator and trustee for 25 years until death 1916.

cross_connections.display_label: Tutored or supervised Anthony's law reading after Hamilton per Wikipedia
Second cousin: Pope Dwight grandchild who chaired Phillips Academy class 1875

narrative_blocks.header: Twelfth president of Yale, grandson of the eighth
narrative_blocks.body: He served as the twelfth President of Yale College from 1886 to 1899, eighty-nine years after his grandfather Pope Dwight had become the college's eighth president in 1795. The two presidencies bookended Yale's nineteenth century — the only grandfather-grandson pair of Yale presidents in the institution's history.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried the Edwards-Dwight theological line
narrative_blocks.body: Born at Norwich CT in 1828 to James Dwight III and Susan Breed, he descended on the Dwight side from the eighteenth-century theologian Jonathan Edwards through his great-grandmother Mary Edwards Dwight. The line of New England Calvinist ministers had run unbroken from Hartford 1636 to New Haven 1916 — his death year.

narrative_blocks.header: Sister married Rev. Richard Hooker in 1846
narrative_blocks.body: His elder sister Aurelia married Rev. Richard Hooker — Yale 1827 Presbyterian minister settled in Macon Georgia — in 1846 when Timothy was eighteen. The marriage united two seventh-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker through entirely different lines and produced the dual-descent son Thomas Hooker Jr.

narrative_blocks.header: Top of Yale 1849, Skull and Bones member
narrative_blocks.body: He entered Yale at sixteen and graduated top of the class of 1849, capturing prizes in mathematics and Latin and joining Skull and Bones — the senior society founded seventeen years earlier in 1832 by William Huntington Russell and Alphonso Taft. He stayed at Yale through the 1850s as Clark Scholar then Tutor before two years abroad at Bonn and Berlin universities.

narrative_blocks.header: Founded Hotchkiss prep school for Yale, 1891
narrative_blocks.body: Five years into his Yale presidency he approached Maria Bissell Hotchkiss — widow of arms inventor Benjamin Hotchkiss who had left her a large inheritance — with a proposition to finance a college preparatory school that would prepare young men for Yale. The Hotchkiss School opened at Lakeville Connecticut that year; he served as trustee for the next twenty-five years until his 1916 death.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-great-grandson of Jonathan Edwards
narrative_blocks.body: His paternal grandfather Pope Dwight was the 8th Yale president; Pope Dwight's mother Mary Edwards was the daughter of Rev. Jonathan Edwards, 3rd president of Princeton. Through five generations of presidential and ministerial succession — Edwards → Pope Dwight → James Dwight → Timothy Dwight V — he carried the New England intellectual line into the 20th century.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Tim V

parents.father_research_notes: Yale 1813 graduate. Named for grandfather Roger Sherman (X02819) — the Founding Father.

cross_connections.description: Great-granddaughter of Roger Sherman, Founding Father and signer of all four founding documents.

narrative_blocks.header: Great-granddaughter of a Founding Father, Roger Sherman
narrative_blocks.body: Her great-grandfather Roger Sherman of Connecticut was the only American to sign all four founding documents — the Continental Association, the Declaration of Independence, the Articles of Confederation, and the United States Constitution. Through her father Roger Sherman Skinner (Yale 1813) she carried that legacy into the Yale presidential household when she married Timothy Dwight V in 1866.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

narrative_blocks.header: Died unmarried at forty in Yale president's house
narrative_blocks.body: Born to incoming Yale president Timothy Dwight V at Norwich CT in 1868, she lived with her parents in New Haven through her father's presidency and retirement years. She died there unmarried in October 1909 at age forty; her father survived her by seven years and her mother by ten.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT

education.notes: Yale BA 1893.
Yale PhD 1895 — two years after BA.
Yale LLB 1896.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Yale degrees, then deferred marriage to forty-five
narrative_blocks.body: He took his Yale BA in 1893, PhD in 1895, and LL.B. in 1896 — three Yale degrees in seven years while his father was sitting Yale president. He waited until age forty-five to marry Mary Van Winkle Ketcham of New York in 1917 and had no children, dying in 1944 at age seventy-two.

cross_connections.notes: No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.
No other family has provided three presidents of Yale. Timothy Dwight College at Yale is named for both the 8th and 12th presidents.

cross_connections.display_label: Called Yale colleague James Luce Kingsley 'the American Addison'

cross_connections.description: Talcott-side descendant given middle name 'Dwight' in 1855, likely after Pope Dwight (8th Yale president); a second Timothy Dwight Hobart (TD0131 b.1826) carries the same name pattern.

narrative_blocks.header: He made Yale the nation’s largest college
narrative_blocks.body: As Yale’s eighth president from 1795 to 1817 he hired Benjamin Silliman as its first chemistry professor — later called the patriarch of American science — and grew Yale into the largest college in the country. His revival sermons there helped touch off the Second Great Awakening.

narrative_blocks.header: He argued for independence a year early
narrative_blocks.body: In July 1775, a full year before the Declaration, he was openly urging the colonies to declare independence — by his own account using the very arguments later adopted — while many around him were still fearful or opposed.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote America’s first epic poem
narrative_blocks.body: In 1785 he published “The Conquest of Canaan,” an eleven-book biblical epic dedicated to George Washington and reckoned the first epic poem written in America. He was a leading “Hartford Wit” alongside John Trumbull, Joel Barlow, and Noah Webster.

narrative_blocks.header: A mission in Arkansas still bears his name
narrative_blocks.body: He helped found the American Board of Commissioners for Foreign Missions in 1810; a decade later, in 1820, its Dwight Mission to the Cherokee in Arkansas was named in his honor. As a Continental Army chaplain in the Revolution he had written the patriotic song “Columbia” for his troops.

narrative_blocks.header: Stolen pears taught him the eighth commandment
narrative_blocks.body: As a small boy he took pears from a neighbor’s yard with older schoolmates; his mother told him it broke the commandment against stealing. He returned them in tears and refused to keep them even when the owner offered — a story he later preached to his Yale congregation.

narrative_blocks.header: He wrote a war anthem as army chaplain
narrative_blocks.body: While serving as a chaplain in the Continental Army, Timothy Dwight wrote the patriotic song "Columbia." Set to music, it became one of the young republic's stirring anthems.

artworks.blurb: John Trumbull oil portrait, painted 1817 — year of Pope Dwight's death.

institutions.institution_blurb: Founding figure; Dwight Mission named in his honor.

institutions.blurb: Taught at Hopkins Grammar School in New Haven after his 1769 Yale graduation, before becoming a Yale Tutor.

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with wife Mary Woolsey, son James Dwight III, granddaughter Aurelia, grandson Tim V

education.notes: Entered Yale at age 13; graduated top of

career.notes: pastor; ran a noted boys’ academy
Revolutionary War chaplain
president, 1795-1817

parents.father_name: Benjamin Woolsey of Long Island NY — was Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend
parents.father_research_notes: Mary's father Benjamin Woolsey predeceased the Dwight marriage. Earlier tree data incorrectly set father_id=H00914 (her HUSBAND Pope Dwight). Corrected Stage 6Y-161.

bio.extra_names: Sometimes Margaret (EH 914 calls her Margaret; FAG and consensus sources Mary; per Sam, the same person — Mary as nickname for Margaret)

burial.plot_notes: Grove Street Cemetery, New Haven CT — with husband Pope Dwight

narrative_blocks.header: Brother and sister-in-law cemented Woolsey-Dwight ties
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Benjamin Woolsey had been Maj. Timothy Dwight III's Yale classmate, roommate, and intimate friend — the Woolsey-Dwight connection preceded her own 1777 marriage to Pope Dwight. Her brother William Walter Woolsey later married Elizabeth Dwight, sister of her husband Pope Dwight, doubling the family link in the next generation.

burial.plot_notes: Unknown grave near Natchez, Adams County, Mississippi — died in the wilderness 1777, body location never recorded

education.notes: Yale 1744.

career.notes: Merchant in Northampton MA. Substantial family business.
Judge 1758-1774. Took oath of fealty to British crown on assuming office — held this oath binding through the Revolution and refused to revoke it.

narrative_blocks.header: Loyalist judge who would not break his oath
narrative_blocks.body: He had sworn fealty to the British crown in 1758 on becoming judge of the court of common pleas, and held that oath binding when the Revolution came. His Northampton family suffered increasing community ostracism as the war approached; one biographer called him 'a Loyalist on Christian principle, and yet thoroughly patriotic in his feelings.'

narrative_blocks.header: Took two sons to a Mississippi colony, 1776
narrative_blocks.body: Disillusioned with revolutionary Northampton, in May 1776 he sailed from Middletown CT with two of his sons — Sereno and Jonathan — toward a British land grant at Natchez Mississippi. They were accompanied by the Phelps and Lyman families and the Rev Mr Smith's household; within a year he and Mrs Lyman would both be dead at Natchez of fever.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in the wilderness, body never recovered
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Natchez Mississippi in June 1777 at age fifty in the wilderness, without trial or recorded grave. News of his death traveled slowly back to Northampton; his son Timothy Dwight IV left the Continental Army in 1778 to return home as the oldest surviving male and take charge of the family's affairs.

narrative_blocks.header: Carried tiny wife on his palm, arm's length
narrative_blocks.body: His wife Mary Edwards Dwight was as small as he was large — he sometimes carried her around the room on his open palm held out at arm's length. She bore him thirteen children, eight of them sons, all noted for fine physical forms — the smallest son Cecil was five-foot-eight-and-a-half and weighed two hundred pounds.

cross_connections.display_label: Refused his Northampton congregation, took communion twelve miles away instead.

narrative_blocks.header: Married at sixteen as Edwards left for Stockbridge
narrative_blocks.body: Her November 1750 wedding to Major Timothy Dwight came two months before Jonathan Edwards moved his family to the Stockbridge mission. The marriage was permitted because the family was about to be separated.

narrative_blocks.header: Mother of Yale's eighth president Timothy Dwight
narrative_blocks.body: Her son Timothy Dwight IV led Yale College from 1795 to 1817. He shaped a generation of New England intellectual life from his grandfather Edwards's old territory.

narrative_blocks.header: Refused her pew when Hooker succeeded her father
narrative_blocks.body: When the distant Thomas Hooker descendant Rev. John Hooker became Northampton's pastor after Edwards's dismissal, she placed her own chair in the vestibule below the belfry. On communion Sabbaths she rode twelve miles on a pillion behind her son to take communion at another church.

narrative_blocks.header: Heard a year later her husband had died
narrative_blocks.body: Major Timothy Dwight III died at Natchez, Mississippi in 1777 during a southern trip. The news did not reach her at Northampton until a full year after his death.

narrative_blocks.header: Husband could carry her on his open palm
narrative_blocks.body: She was unusually small in stature and was described in old age as 'opinionated and self-willed.' Patriot rabble burned her fields and stole her livestock during the Revolution because of her husband's Tory sympathies.

burialCemetery.name: Grove Street Cemetery
burialCemetery.notes: Historic New Haven cemetery. Hezekiah Stanley (d. Sept 11, 1860) and Sally Hooker Stanley (d. Dec 21, 1862) buried here.
